generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee at my window, the sea roiling as if it too knows the weight of this moment. The lantern is lit, as it must be, though I fear the light may not reach the shore this night. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to write, as though the ink might carry my words to you across the miles and the years.

You were once a child of light, Eleanor. I remember the way you would stand on the cliffs, eyes wide with wonder, watching the waves crash against the rocks. You would ask me questions that made me pause—why the sea was so wild, why the sky was so dark, why I never laughed like the other men. I told you stories, of sailors lost to the deep, of storms that swallowed ships whole, of the loneliness that comes with a life of salt and stone. But I never told you of the loneliness that comes with a life without you.

I have not seen you since the day you left. Not in person, not in letter, not in the silence that has grown between us. I have tried, Eleanor. I have written, I have waited, I have even sent a few trinkets from the lighthouse—small things, like a shell or a piece of driftwood, hoping they might remind you of the place where

My Dearest Clara,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may claim me before dawn. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if this letter will ever reach you, but I must try.

You were but a child when you left, and I did not understand why. You told me you were going to find your own way, to become a doctor, to see the world. I did not know how to hold you back, and perhaps I should have. You were so full of fire and purpose, and I, a man of stone and salt, could not match your light.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, not for glory, but because it is my duty. I have watched the tides, the storms, the endless cycle of the sea. I have seen ships sink and souls fade, and I have learned to live with the silence. But tonight, the silence is heavy, and I fear it will be my end.

I have not seen your mother since the day you left. She has grown frail, and I have not been able to visit. I do not know if she still waits for you, or if she has given up. I have not written to you, and I do not know if you will read this, but I must tell you this: I have always

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the lighthouse walls. The sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the storm in my own heart. I have not seen you in five years, and yet tonight, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write.

You were once my pride, my light in the darkest hours. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes burned with defiance and your voice with sorrow. I could not bear to see you go, but I did not dare stop you. You were young, full of fire, and I feared that if I held you back, I would lose you forever. So I let you go, hoping that time would soften the edges of your anger and bring us back to one another.

But time has not been kind. It has only deepened the chasm between us. I have spent these years watching the sea, the waves, the stars—never once thinking of you. I told myself that you would come back when you were ready, but I have waited in vain. And now, as this storm approaches, I am not certain I will see another dawn.

I have always believed in the power of the light, Eleanor. The lighthouse is my duty, my purpose. But tonight, I feel the weight of the world pressing upon me. I am not the man I once was, and I fear that the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea roaring as though it would devour the very foundations of this rock. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end as kindly as the dawn. Yet, I must write—though I know you may not read these words, and I know you may not care. But I must try, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not held you in my arms since you were but a child.

You were once the light of my life, Eleanor. I remember the day you were born, how the sea seemed to hush its fury, as if the very heavens were smiling upon me. You were a small, fierce thing, with eyes like the stars above, and a laugh that could melt the coldest winter. I used to tell you stories of the sea, of mermaids and shipwrecks, and of the great lighthouse that would guide the lost to safety. You would press your small hand against the glass of the lantern, as if trying to reach through it to the world beyond.

But time, like the tide, has taken its course. You left, and I have not heard from you since. I do not know why you chose to turn your back on me, nor do I care to ask. You were always a spirited child, and perhaps you thought the world was too

**April 14th, 1892**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the souls of the damned in the graveyard beyond the cliffs. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and grey. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but I must speak, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and the silence has grown heavier than the stones of this lighthouse.

I have always believed that a lighthouse must stand firm, even in the worst of storms. But I fear that I have become more like the rocks of the shore—cold, unyielding, and, perhaps, unloving. I have not spoken to you in years, and I have not sent a single letter. I told myself it was for your own good, that you were too young to understand the weight of a father's heart, and that the sea would one day calm, and we would meet again. But the sea has not calmed, and I have not spoken.

You were a child when you left, Eliza. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too brave for her years. I remember the day you stood at the door, your mother’s hand in yours, and you looked at me as if I were a stranger. I did not know how to answer your questions. I did not know how to explain that I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. Yet tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury, I find myself compelled to reach out—not to mend what has been broken, but to say, at the very least, that I am here, still watching, still waiting.

The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than just my life. I have spent my days in this lonely tower, keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark waters, and for those who might be lost in the darkness of their own souls. But tonight, the skies are black, and the waves rise like giants, threatening to devour the very foundation of this place.

I know you do not want to hear from me. I know you have chosen your own path, and I have chosen mine. But I cannot help but think of you in the midst of this chaos. I have seen your name in the newspapers, heard whispers of your life in the city, and I have felt the ache of your absence like a storm that never passes. You were once my light, Eleanor, and I fear I have let it fade.

I do not ask for forgiveness, nor do I beg for your return. I only ask that you know, in

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that has long since stopped beating in time with yours. Five years have passed since the last letter, and I have counted every day, every hour, every minute of silence. The sea has been my only companion these past years, and the wind has whispered your name in the night. But I have been a fool, Eleanor, and I have let pride and shame keep me from reaching out.

You were only ten when we last spoke, and even then, I could see the fire in your eyes—fierce, unyielding, like the storm that now rages against these cliffs. I remember the way you looked at me, not with anger, but with something deeper, something I could not name. Perhaps it was sorrow. Perhaps it was disappointment. Perhaps it was the weight of a father who had failed you.

I have not been a good man, Eleanor. I have let the loneliness of this life harden me, and I have let the burden of my sins weigh down my soul. I have not spoken to you because I feared your forgiveness, and I have not sought it because I feared I did not deserve it. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the sea howls like a beast, I find myself questioning whether I will live to see another dawn.

You were right to leave. You were right to walk away from a man

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has worn the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the pain of our parting. I have often wondered if you still remember me at all, or if the memory of your father has been reduced to nothing more than a shadow in your past.

Tonight, the wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea roars with a fury that I have never known. The lighthouse stands tall, but I fear it may not stand much longer. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see dawn.

I have spent my life watching the horizon, guiding ships through the darkness, but never have I felt so lost as I do now. The light I once held so dear is flickering, and I wonder if I am the only one who has ever truly seen it.

I have thought of you often, Eliza. I have remembered the days we spent by the sea, the way your laughter echoed across the waves, and the way your eyes sparkled like the stars above. I have wished, more than once, that I had been the one to hold you close, to offer you comfort, not conflict.

If I do not survive this night, I hope you will not hold it against me. I have made my share of mistakes, and I pray that in time

**April 12th, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea has been restless all day, and now the sky is a bruised shade of black, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, though tonight I fear it may be the last time. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not a single word of explanation. I thought you had been taken by the sea, like the others. But no, you were here, in this very town, and you chose to walk away. I do not know why, and I do not know if I ever will. But I write this not for answers, but for you.

I have always believed in the light, Eliza. Not just the lamp that guides the ships, but the light that guides the soul. I have spent my life standing here, watching over the waves, believing that there is always a way through the darkness. I have taught the children who come to visit, I have shared the stories of sailors who found their way home, and I have held the lantern high, even when the world seemed to spin out of control

**April 1st, 1897**  
**The Lighthouse, North Point**  
**North Sea, England**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee at my back, the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very cliffs we once stood upon. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end with me still here to tell the tale. But if I should survive—though I do not know if I will—then this letter must reach you.  

Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I have not spoken a word to you since. I know I have been cruel, and I have no excuse for it. You were a child when you left, and I was a man hardened by the sea, by grief, by the weight of my own failures. I did not know how to reach out, nor how to mend what had been broken. I thought silence would be easier. I was wrong.  

I have watched the waves for many years, Eleanor. I have watched the sky turn dark with the promise of storms, and I have learned to read the signs. But tonight, the sky does not look like any storm I have seen before. The air is thick with a cold that does not belong to the sea, and the wind carries a sound I do not recognize. It is not the sound of the sea, nor of the land. It

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. The sea has been restless for days, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I write this with trembling hands, for the storm is upon us, and I am not certain I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I have not written, either, for I feared the words would not reach you, or worse, that you would not wish to receive them. But now, as the tempest gathers strength, I feel compelled to speak, to say what has long been unspoken.

You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the lighthouse, though that is what I tend to every night, every morning, every dusk. You were my light in the darkest hours of my life—when the world seemed to have turned its back on me, and I was left alone with the sea and the silence of the night. You were my daughter, my heart, my everything.

And yet, you left. You did not write, you did not call, and you did not come back. I tried to forgive you, but I could not. I could not forgive the way you walked out of my life like the tide had taken you away. I could not forgive the silence that followed, the coldness that settled in my bones, the emptiness that no amount of sea salt

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I am. I write to you from the lighthouse, where the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear for my life, but I cannot let this moment pass without speaking to you.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not dared to write. I know you would not have answered, and I suppose I have not deserved it. But tonight, as the world outside threatens to end, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. Perhaps it is the loneliness of the sea, or perhaps it is the weight of my own sins, but I cannot shake the feeling that I have been neglecting you for far too long.

I have always believed that duty is the highest calling, and I have spent my life in service to this light, guiding ships through the darkness. But I have neglected the light within my own heart, and I have allowed that light to grow dim. I have not been there for you when you needed me, and I have not spoken the words that might have mended what was broken between us.

You were a child when I last saw you, and I was a man who had forgotten how to be a father. I was too busy with the sea, too busy with the duty that I believed was more important than anything else. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of desperation, my hands trembling not from the cold, but from the weight of words I’ve long buried. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the echo of your footsteps still lingers in the hollow of this lighthouse. The sea has not spared me, nor has time. Tonight, a storm unlike any I’ve seen in my years of keeping this light—its winds howl like the ghosts of the drowned, and the waves rise as if to claim the very stones of this cliff.

I’ve spent my life watching the sea, guiding ships through its moods, and yet I have never been able to guide you back. You were always the one who sought the horizon, while I remained tethered to the shore. I have no regrets, not truly—but I do regret that the last time we spoke, it was with words that cut deeper than the tide.

You once said I was a man of stone, unmoved by the world’s sorrows. Perhaps you were right. I have always been more a keeper of the light than a father. But tonight, as the wind screams through the cracks in this old tower, I find myself questioning whether I have been a fool to let the light go unlit in my own heart.

I have no grand apologies, no sweeping declarations of love. Only this: I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not stopped watching for your return. And if

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the courage to write, yet the storm outside has stirred something in me I cannot ignore. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it too knows the weight of the night. I have stood on these cliffs for thirty years, weathered more storms than I can count, but tonight feels different. It is not the wind or the waves that unsettle me, but the silence of your absence. Five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years.

You were ten when you left, and I did not stop you. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world was too cruel, too harsh for a child of your tender soul. But I was wrong, and I have spent every day since mourning the daughter I lost. I have not spoken of you, not even in my thoughts, for fear that if I did, I might find myself reaching for you, and you would not be there. You would not be there, as you are not.

I have heard from your mother. She sends me letters, always in the same neat hand, with the same careful words. She says you are well, that you have found your own path, and that you are happy. I believe her, for I have no reason to doubt her. But I also know that happiness is a fragile thing, and I fear that the path you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since I last saw you, and though the days have worn on, the ache of your absence has never truly faded. I have spent these years in the solitude of this lighthouse, watching the sea with a gaze that no longer holds the same purpose it once did. The light still burns, but it is I who have grown dim.

Tonight, a storm is coming—one like none I have seen in my time. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves rise with a fury that speaks of something far older than mere weather. I have prepared the lamp, secured the windows, and made my peace with the inevitable. Yet, as I sit here, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years.

I know you would not believe me if I told you this, but I have never stopped thinking of you. I have never stopped hoping that one day, you might return. I have never stopped wondering if you still carry the same anger in your heart that you left with when you left this place. I know I was not the father you wanted, and I know I have done wrong. I have let the sea and my own stubborn pride take the place of the man I once was.

I have no grand words to offer you, no great apologies, only the truth of a man who has spent his life in the company

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the window of this old lighthouse, the wind howling like the wails of a restless soul, and I find myself compelled to write to you. It has been five long years since I last saw your face, and now, with the storm gathering strength in the east, I fear I may not live to see another dawn. The sea roars with a fury I have not known in my years here, and the light, which I have tended so faithfully, flickers like a dying ember.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and I do not know if my words will reach you, or if you will even read them. But I must try. I have spent these years in quiet torment, watching the waves crash against the rocks, wondering if you ever think of me, if you ever remember the days we shared before the rift between us. I remember the last time we spoke, the way your voice trembled with anger, and how I, in my pride, refused to yield. I was wrong, and I have paid for it in silence.

You were a child then, and I was a fool. I let my stubbornness blind me to the pain I caused you. I should have listened, I should have loved you more. Now, as I face the storm, I am reminded of the tempests that rage upon the sea—unrelenting, merciless, and often beyond our control

**April 12th, 1892**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the strength of his hand, and the heart of a man who has long since lost the strength of his soul. The sea howls outside my window, a sound that has grown familiar to me over these many years, yet tonight it feels different. It is not merely the wind that stirs the air, but the weight of the storm that threatens to swallow the world whole, and with it, perhaps, my last breath.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you. The days have grown long and weary, the nights longer still, and the silence of your absence has settled deep into the bones of this old lighthouse. I have spoken to no one but the sea, and even it has grown quiet in recent days, as though it, too, senses the coming tempest.

I have not written to you, Eliza. I have not dared. The words have always felt too heavy, too sharp, and I feared that if I spoke them, they would cut deeper than the salt that has long since dried upon my skin. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the wind begins to howl like a beast in pain, I feel that I must try once more.

You were a child when you left, and I was

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that brews beyond the cliffs, and with hands that tremble not from the wind, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have lived with the silence of a son-in-law’s cold gaze and the absence of your laughter, which once filled these halls like the sea at dawn.

I do not know if I shall see another morning. The sky is dark with gathering fury, and the waves grow wilder by the moment. The lighthouse, though built to withstand the harshest tempests, is but a fragile thing in the face of nature’s wrath. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for every storm, but tonight feels different. The wind howls like a beast, and the sea roars with a voice that sounds almost human.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I have not written. I have not called. And yet, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I have spent those years in the quiet of this lonely place, watching the waves, counting the hours, and wondering if I had done enough to deserve your love, or if I had done too much, and lost you in the process.

I know you were angry. I know you thought I had chosen duty over family. But I did not choose it lightly, nor did

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in better spirits than I have been in recent years. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though I have tried to forget the pain of our last parting, time has only made it more difficult to let go. I write to you now not out of desperation, but from a place of quiet resolve—though I fear I may not live to see the dawn of this day.

The storm is coming, and it is unlike any I have seen in my years here on this rocky shore. The sea has been restless all week, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I know the light may not be enough to guide the ships through the chaos. I have done my duty, as I always have, but I cannot help but wonder if I have done my duty to you as well.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I know I have not been the father you deserved. I was blind to the pain you carried, and I was too proud to admit my own. I should have been there when you needed me most, and I was not. I should have reached out, and I did not. I can only hope that this letter will reach you before it is too late.

I have not told you of the nightmares that haunt me each night. I see your face in the fog, and I hear

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast upon the rocks, and the sea roiling as though it seeks to swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may claim me before dawn. Yet, in the midst of this chaos, my thoughts turn not to the tempest, but to you—my daughter, my only child.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have long since ceased to hope for a reply. But tonight, as the lighthouse stands alone against the fury of the heavens, I find myself compelled to reach out, not for forgiveness, but for the sake of my own peace.

I have spent these years in solitude, tending to the beacon, watching the waves, and remembering the days when our home was filled with laughter and the scent of fresh bread. I have not forgotten the way you used to dance in the garden, or the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of your dreams. I have not forgotten the way you once called me "Father," with such love and certainty.

And yet, I know you have not forgotten me either. I have seen it in the way you look at the sea, in the way you speak of the stars. You carry a piece of me within you, just as I carry a piece of you in my heart.

If you are reading this, I beg you—do not let the silence between us grow any longer

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, its beam slicing through the gathering storm, and I am reminded of the last time I saw you—five long years ago. The sea was calm that day, the sky a brilliant blue, and yet, even then, I felt the weight of the world pressing down on my chest. I was not ready to let you go, but you were not ready to stay. You left with the wind, and I was left with the silence.

I have written many letters to you over the years, each one lost to the sea or the fire. I told myself that one day, when the storm had passed and the waves had calmed, I would find you again. But time, like the tides, has a way of eroding even the strongest of resolutions. I have grown old, Eleanor. The years have worn me down, and the sea, ever cruel, has taken more than I can bear.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The wind howls like a beast, and the waves rise like the hands of a vengeful god. I have seen the sky darken with a fury I have not felt since the day you left. I do not know if I will make it through this night. The lighthouse is old, and I am older. I have always been the keeper of this light, but I fear that tonight, the light may be the only thing that remains of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the easiest of missives to receive. I write to you now not with the calm of a clear sky, but under the weight of a storm that threatens to swallow the sea and the world as we know it. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, in its fury, seems to roar with the very memory of all that has passed between us.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought you back. I have not spoken to you in that time, not because I do not wish to, but because I feared the words would not be enough. They would have been too little, too late, and I would have only made it worse.

I have always been a man of duty, Eliza. That is what I have lived by—my duty to the light, to the sea, to the lives that depend upon it. I have stood here, night after night, through storms and stillness, through joy and sorrow, through the silence of the world. And yet, I have never once thought of you. Not truly. Not until now.

The storm is coming, and I do not know if I will see the dawn. I have been told that the lighthouse is strong, that it will stand through the gales. But I am not the lighthouse. I am but a man, frail

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and grey. I do not know if I shall see morning. But I must write, for if I do not, I fear I shall be lost in this storm, both of body and soul.

Five years have passed since you left. Five long, bitter years. I have kept the light burning every night, as I have always done, but I have not seen your face in the glass, nor heard your voice in the silence. The loneliness has grown like a vine, creeping up my spine and wrapping around my heart. I have tried to fill the void with work, with the sea, with the distant cries of gulls—but none of it has ever been enough.

I remember the last time we spoke. You stood at the door, your eyes like cold steel, and told me you could not stay. You said I had never been a father to you. That I had never been there when you needed me. And I could not argue. I had not been there. I had been too busy with the light, too wrapped up in the sea, too lost in my own thoughts to see the pain you carried.

But I did not know. I did not know that the light was not enough for you, that the sea was not enough, that the silence of

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a banshee, rattling the shutters of this old keep as if to mimic the storm of silence that has passed between us. I've spent the last five years in this lighthouse, watching the sea with a steady light, and yet I've never felt so alone as I do tonight.

I have not seen you in five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have tried, I truly have tried, to reach you, to write, to call, to send word that I am still here, still watching over the waters that you once called home. But you have not answered. You have not returned my letters. You have not come back, and I have not gone to you. We have both been too proud, too stubborn, too afraid.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steadfast, unshaken by the tempests of the world. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the sea roars with fury, I find myself questioning whether I am truly as strong as I once thought. The waves crash against the rocks with a violence that is almost human, and I fear that this storm may take more than just the light. I fear that it may take me.

I have no regrets, not for the years I have spent alone, not for the choices I have made

**The Lighthouse, Point Evershade**  
**October 13th, 1897**  

**My Dearest Eleanor,**  

If you are reading these words, it means I have survived another night, though not without peril. The sea has been restless all evening, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The lantern’s light is steady, as it always has been, but I fear the storm may be too great. I write this with trembling hands, for the gales have grown fiercer, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that shakes the very bones of this old tower.  

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have not written, nor sent a single letter. You must know that I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the way your mother’s eyes wept as you walked through the door, her hand gripping yours as if to keep you from vanishing into the night. I remember the way your father’s voice cracked as he said, *“You must go, Eleanor. For your own good.”*  

I have tried to forget, but the sea does not forget. It whispers your name in the wind, and I have heard it often. I have seen your face in the mist, in the flicker of the lantern’s flame. I have felt your absence in every wave that breaks upon these rocks.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks in this old stone tower. The sea is restless, and the sky is black as a thief’s heart. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but if I do, I must speak these words—words I have held in my chest for five long, cruel years.

You were twelve when you left. Twelve. A child. And yet, you walked away from me like a woman, with your head held high and your eyes like cold steel. I have not seen your face in five years, not once. I have not heard your voice, not once. You left me with a note, penned in that same hand that once traced my name in the sand. “I cannot live in the shadow of your disappointment, Father.” That was your last word to me. And I have never forgotten it.

I do not know what you saw in me that made you believe I would ever disappoint you. I have spent my life in this lighthouse, through storms fiercer than this, through nights when the sea rose like a vengeful god. I have watched the dawn break over these cliffs, and I have held the light for countless souls who would have drowned in the dark. And yet, you did not stay. You did not trust me. You did not believe I would ever love you as you deserved.
